attributes.stem:
A 8-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parent concerned about a patch of 'blackheads' on his upper back that has been present since early childhood. The lesion is asymptomatic but causes him embarrassment when swimming. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a well-demarcated 4 cm patch of closely grouped open comedones on his upper back. The surrounding skin appears normal with no evidence of inflammation or infection. There is no comedones on his face or elsewhere on his body.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features typical of a comedonal naevus (naevus comedonicus), which is a rare developmental abnormality characterised by grouped comedones or plugs with a honeycomb appearance in a localised area anywhere on the body. Unlike acne vulgaris, these lesions are present from birth or early childhood and remain localised to a specific area. Usually it is singular and benign, however there can be a group which is associated with neurological, skeletal and ophthalmological abnormalities called naevus comedonicus syndrome.

Topical tretinoin is the most appropriate first-line treatment for comedonal naevus. It helps to reduce the appearance of comedones by increasing cell turnover and preventing follicular plugging. This is a reasonable first step before considering more invasive options, especially in a young patient.

Benzoyl peroxide cream is commonly used for acne vulgaris but is less effective for comedonal naevus as the pathophysiology differs from typical acne. The comedones in this condition are due to a developmental abnormality rather than inflammation or bacterial colonisation.

Oral isotretinoin would be excessive for this condition. It carries significant side effects and is typically reserved for severe, widespread acne. This localised developmental condition does not warrant such aggressive systemic therapy.

Immediate surgical excision is overly aggressive as a first-line treatment. While excision may eventually be considered if conservative measures fail, it should not be the initial approach given the potential for scarring and the availability of less invasive options.

Regular extraction of comedones would provide only temporary improvement and could lead to scarring. It does not address the underlying developmental abnormality and would need to be repeated frequently.

The management approach should be guided by the patient's symptoms and cosmetic concerns. If topical tretinoin proves ineffective after an adequate trial, other options including laser therapy or surgical excision could be considered. Patient education about the benign nature of the condition is also important.
learningPoints: Comedonal naevus is a rare developmental abnormality characterised by grouped comedones in a localised area.
Comedonal naevus is benign and does not require treatment unless causing psychological distress or recurrent infection.
references: Sivasaththive M, Keefe M, Mitchell G. Comedo naevus. DermNet NZ. 2022. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/comedo-naevus
Yadav P, Mendiratta V, Rana S, Chander R. Nevus Comedonicus Syndrome. Indian J Dermatol. 2015. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4533567/
